transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Patrick. I’m glad you’re here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi, thanks for having me. I guess... I’ve been feeling a bit overwhelmed lately, especially at work. There’s this constant feeling that I’m not doing enough, you know? It’s like no matter how hard I try, it never seems to meet the expectations. It’s been weighing on me a lot.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy weight with those feelings of being overwhelmed and not meeting expectations at work. That must be really challenging. Can you tell me more about what specific situations at work are contributing to that feeling?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s mostly when I encounter micromanagement. It feels like every little thing I do is being scrutinized, and it just makes me second-guess myself. I start to think that maybe I’m not cut out for this job. It’s frustrating because I want to do well, but it feels like I’m always falling short. I find myself going over past experiences in my head, wondering if I could have done something differently.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like micromanagement really intensifies your feelings of self-doubt and frustration. It’s understandable to feel that way when you’re under such close scrutiny. You mentioned going over past experiences and wondering if you could have done things differently. Can you share more about what those thoughts look like for you? What kind of experiences come to mind?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Well, I guess I often think about times when I felt like I was doing well, but then I got feedback that made me question everything. Like, I remember a project where I thought I had put in a lot of effort, but then my manager pointed out a few mistakes. Instead of focusing on what I did right, I just fixated on those errors. It’s like I can’t help but replay those moments in my mind, thinking maybe if I had just done something differently, it would have turned out better. It’s hard to shake off that feeling of inadequacy.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It seems like those moments of feedback really stick with you, overshadowing the effort and successes you’ve had. That fixation on mistakes can create a loop of self-doubt and inadequacy. I can imagine how exhausting that must be. When you think about those experiences, what emotions come up for you? How do you feel in those moments of reflection?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s definitely exhausting. When I reflect on those experiences, I often feel anxious and worried. There’s this tightness in my chest, and I start to feel scared about what others might think of me. I also feel kind of sad and lonely, like I’m the only one struggling with this. It’s tough because I want to be competent and confident, but those thoughts just keep creeping in, making me feel down and unhappy. It’s like I’m stuck in this cycle, and I don’t know how to break free from it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds really heavy, Patrick. The anxiety and worry, along with that sense of loneliness, can be really isolating. It’s understandable to want to feel competent and confident, especially when you’re putting in so much effort. The cycle you’re describing—where those negative thoughts keep creeping in—can feel really hard to escape. When you think about breaking free from that cycle, what do you feel might be holding you back the most?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think a big part of what holds me back is this fear of failure. I worry that if I try to change things or speak up about how I feel, it might just make things worse. There’s this voice in my head that tells me I’m not good enough, and it makes me hesitant to take risks or be more open about my struggles. I guess I also feel like I don’t want to burden others with my issues, so I keep a lot of it to myself. It’s like I’m caught between wanting to improve and being afraid of what that might mean for me.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That fear of failure and the voice telling you that you’re not good enough can be really powerful and paralyzing. It’s understandable that you’d hesitate to speak up or take risks when it feels like there’s so much at stake. It’s also compassionate of you to consider how your struggles might affect others, but it sounds like that’s adding to your burden. When you think about wanting to improve while feeling afraid, what do you think would help you feel more comfortable taking those steps?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it would help to have a bit more support, maybe someone to talk to who understands what I’m going through. Just knowing that I’m not alone in this could make a difference. I also wonder if I could find ways to challenge those negative thoughts, maybe by reminding myself of the times I have succeeded or received positive feedback. But it’s hard to put that into practice when I’m feeling so overwhelmed. I guess I just need to find a way to build my confidence back up, but I’m not quite sure how to start.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like having support and connection with someone who understands could really help you feel less alone in your experience. That’s a valuable insight. Challenging those negative thoughts by recalling your successes is a great idea, but I can see how it might feel daunting when you’re overwhelmed. Building your confidence back up is a journey, and it’s okay to take small steps. What do you think would be the first small step you could take toward building that support or challenging those thoughts?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Maybe the first step could be reaching out to a colleague I trust. I’ve noticed a few people who seem to understand the pressures we face at work, and it might help to just talk things through with one of them. I think sharing my feelings could lighten the load a bit. As for challenging my thoughts, I could try keeping a journal where I write down my successes, no matter how small. Just having a space to reflect on those moments might help me see things differently. It feels a bit scary to think about, but I guess it’s a start.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those sound like meaningful first steps, Patrick. Reaching out to a trusted colleague could provide that support and connection you’re looking for, and it’s great that you’re considering sharing your feelings. Keeping a journal to reflect on your successes, no matter how small, could also be a powerful way to shift your perspective over time. It’s completely normal to feel a bit scared about taking these steps, but it sounds like you’re open to trying. How do you feel about setting a timeline for when you might reach out or start journaling?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think setting a timeline could help me feel more accountable. Maybe I could aim to reach out to that colleague by the end of this week. It feels manageable, and having a specific time in mind might make it easier to actually do it. As for the journaling, I could start that tonight—just a few minutes to jot down some thoughts. I think having those small goals could help me feel like I’m making progress, even if it’s just a little at a time.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a solid plan, Patrick. Setting a goal to reach out by the end of the week feels manageable and gives you a clear target. Starting your journaling tonight for just a few minutes is a great way to ease into it without feeling overwhelmed. It’s wonderful that you’re focusing on those small goals—they can really help create a sense of progress. How are you feeling about these steps now that you’ve outlined them?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel a bit more hopeful, actually. Just having a plan in place makes it seem less daunting. I think knowing that I’m taking these small steps gives me a sense of control, which is something I’ve been missing. It’s still a little scary, but I’m starting to feel like maybe I can handle this. I guess it’s a step toward breaking that cycle of self-doubt, and that feels encouraging. Thank you for helping me sort through this.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: I’m really glad to hear that you’re feeling more hopeful, Patrick. Having a plan and a sense of control can make a big difference, especially when you’re navigating those feelings of self-doubt. It’s encouraging to see you recognize that these small steps are a way to break that cycle. Remember, it’s okay to take things at your own pace. If you ever want to talk more about how these steps are going or anything else on your mind, I’m here for you. What do you think would be helpful to focus on in our next conversation?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you, I appreciate that. I think it would be helpful to talk about how those steps go, especially after I reach out to my colleague and start journaling. I’d like to reflect on how I feel after taking those actions and see if there are any new insights or challenges that come up. It might also be good to explore more about those negative thoughts and how to manage them as I try to build my confidence. I’m looking forward to our next conversation.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a great plan, Patrick. Reflecting on your experiences after taking those steps will be valuable, and it’ll be helpful to explore any new insights or challenges that arise. We can definitely dive deeper into managing those negative thoughts and building your confidence as well. I’m looking forward to hearing about your progress and supporting you along the way. Take care, and I’ll be here when you’re ready to talk again.

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you, I really appreciate your support. I’ll take care and look forward to our next conversation. It feels good to have someone to talk to about all of this. See you soon!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re very welcome, Patrick. I’m glad to be here for you, and I look forward to our next conversation as well. Take care of yourself, and remember to be gentle with yourself as you take these steps. See you soon!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you, I will keep that in mind. I’ll do my best to be gentle with myself. See you soon!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a wonderful approach, Patrick. Take care, and I’ll be here whenever you’re ready to talk again. See you soon!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I appreciate it. I’ll see you soon!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You’re welcome! Looking forward to our next conversation. Take care, and see you soon!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Looking forward to it too! Take care, and see you soon!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Take care, Patrick! See you soon!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Take care! See you soon!
